title-group.article-title: Climate Change and the Impact On Interstitial Lung Diseases

sec.title: Purpose of Review
sec.p: This review aims to summarize the latest evidence on how climate change has altered the environmental exposures and their influence on the epidemiology, pathophysiology, and outcomes of interstitial lung diseases (ILD).

sec.title: Summary
sec.p: Rising global temperatures are exacerbating environmental threats (like heatwaves, floods, and dust storms) and worsening air quality. This burden disproportionately affects certain vulnerable groups, accelerating the decline of their ILD. Epigenetic modifications play a vital role in explaining the interaction between the environmental factors and development and progression of ILD. Establishment of strong policies is critical for both reducing the rate of climate change and implementing better adaptation strategies to protect the vulnerable group from its ongoing consequences.
Keywords: Interstitial lung diseases, Climate change, Air pollution, Particulate matter
Received 2025 Nov 24; Accepted 2026 Jan 14; Collection date 2026 Mar.

tr.td: Climate change is increasingly linked to the onset, progression, and exacerbation of interstitial lung diseases (ILD) through rising temperatures, extreme weather events, and deteriorating air quality.

Air pollution, heat and humidity, wildfires, dust storms, and shifting aeroallergen patterns significantly influence ILD risk and disease behavior, disproportionately affecting vulnerable populations.
Environmental exposures can accelerate epigenetic aging and activate pathways that drive inflammation and fibrosis in ILD.
Major research gaps remain in understanding gene–environment interactions and the impact of emerging pollutants on ILD.
Strong mitigation policies and adaptive health strategies are urgently needed to safeguard ILD patients in a rapidly changing climate.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Climate change, characterized by rising global temperatures, frequent and intense heat waves, floods, wildfires, and worsened air quality, is increasingly recognized as one of the most significant threat to global health [1]. While its effects on cardiovascular system and respiratory diseases like asthma, allergic rhinitis, chronic obstructive pulmonary diseases, lung cancer are well documented, emerging evidence suggests a link between climate-related factors and the onset, progression and exacerbation of interstitial lung diseases (ILD) [2–6]. ILD are a heterogeneous group of conditions affecting the lung interstitium, characterized by inflammation and/or fibrosis, having varied etiologies, presentations, and treatment [7]. Although genetic predisposition and occupational antigens are established contributors, environmental exposures profoundly shape disease progression [8, 9]. The aim of this review is to critically examine the causal relationship between air pollution-related and climate-associated environmental exposures and ILD, with a focused analysis of combined exposure effects, key confounders, and methodological challenges in causal inference, while highlighting implications for risk mitigation and clinical practice. This article is based on previously conducted studies and does not contain any new studies with human participants or animals performed by any of the authors.

sec.title: Epidemiology
sec.p: The Global Burden of Disease Study 2019 evaluated trends in the prevalence, morbidity, and mortality data on ILD over the three decades from 1990 to 2019, and its findings demonstrated a substantial increase in the global burden of ILD and pulmonary sarcoidosis, with cases rising from 157,441 in 1990 to 390,267 in 2021. Over this period, age-standardized incidence, mortality, and disability-adjusted life years (DALYs) increased annually by 0.61%, 1.32%, and 0.83%, respectively [10]. A rise in prevalence from 52.66 per 100,000 to 57.62 per 100,000 was also noted [11]. The rate of DALYs and mortality also showed uphill trends from 41.57 per 100,000 to 46.45 per 100,000 and 1.76 per 100,000 to 2.17 per 100,000, respectively. Marked demographic differences were observed, with high socioeconomic status areas reporting a higher prevalence, whereas low-to-middle socioeconomic strata areas carried a disproportionately higher mortality and morbidity. Overall, a higher prevalence, DALYs, and mortality was seen among men and older adults. The global trends of ILD are expected to stabilize with minimum variation and remain elevated through 2030 [11]. The demographic disparity highlighted could be driven by a combination of advanced urbanization, better healthcare facilities, and higher aging population in regions identified with high socioeconomic settings (North America, western Europe, and sections of East Asia). Large-scale advanced industrialization increases the exposure to environmental pollutants and occupational hazards which may elevate the incidence of ILD. There is higher public health awareness and people have better access to healthcare centers providing screening facilities, all of which further contribute to a higher and often early detection of ILD. Additionally, a robust healthcare infrastructure allows a significant population of elderly citizens, thereby contributing to a larger ‘at risk’ pool for ILD which is strongly age-related. Parts of sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia, identified as low socioeconomic strata areas, have poor healthcare access and weak facilities [12]. This leads to a delayed diagnosis of ILD, often at an advanced stage, with patients having limited access to proper treatment and necessary supportive care, all of which contribute to a higher mortality rate. The ILD India registry data also highlighted a significant delay in the diagnosis of ILD, a period in which potentially reversible inflammatory disease may progress to fibrotic irreversible forms [13]. In 2021, the highest age-standardized incidence, mortality, and disability rates for ILD were observed in parts of Latin America, in particular Peru and Bolivia. This elevated burden likely reflects the combined effects of limited healthcare capacity and high exposure to environmental pollutants, especially air pollution, which remains a major and under-recognized contributor to ILD risk and adverse outcomes in the region [14]. The available evidence remains limited, with a notable scarcity of data from low- and middle-income countries, regions that are often the most vulnerable to, yet least equipped to cope with, the impacts of climate change.

sec.title: Pathophysiology and Epigenetics
sec.p: A primary risk factor for the initial development and subsequent worsening of fibrotic ILD is advanced age. At a molecular level, key characteristics seen in patients with pulmonary fibrosis and premature aging are shorter telomeres [15–17], accumulation of senescent cells [18], and changes in the epigenome [19]. Epigenetics refers to changes in gene expression which can be influenced by environmental or heritable factors, without altering the underlying DNA sequence [20]. Epigenetic age acceleration is found to be a crucial factor associated with poorer survival in fibrotic ILD (fILD) patients. The median epigenetic age difference (EAD) (the difference between predicted epigenetic age and chronological age) was found to be strikingly higher (11.7 years older) than the chronological age in patients with fILD [21]. An increased exposure to particulate matter (PM) less than 2.5 µ (PM2.5) and poor socioeconomic factors were significantly linked to higher EAD. Specifically, every interquartile range increase in PM2.5​ exposure was associated with an average increase of 2.88 years in accelerated epigenetic age. Increased EAD was also strongly associated with worse transplant-free survival and was a more robust predictor of survival than chronological age alone [21].

The main epigenetic mechanism (Fig. 1) implicated in pathogenesis of ILD is DNA methylation, i.e., the addition of a methyl group to DNA. Aberrant DNA methylation patterns have been widely studied in idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF) and can lead to the silencing or activation of genes involved in inflammation, cellular senescence, and fibrosis. A similar mechanism is also proposed in the pathogenesis of air pollution-induced development of hypersensitivity pneumonitis (HP) [22]. Other less common epigenetic mechanisms include histone modification and non-coding RNAs which are postulated to promote fibroblast proliferation in IPF [23–26].

caption.p: Proposed epigenetic mechanisms contributing to the pathogenesis of interstitial lung disease as a consequence of climate change. Vehicular emission of noxious gases, wildfire smoke, greenhouse gas emissions, and uncontrolled construction activities release polluted gases into the atmosphere, and these gases, when inhaled, lead to DNA methylation (predominant mechanism), histone modification and formation of non-coding RNAs which create a profibrotic milieu causing fibroblast proliferation leading to idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis

sec.p: While many in vitro and in vivo studies unfold the effect of air pollution-induced epigenetic changes, data on patients with ILD are scarce. A study by Goobie et al. found that higher PM2.5​ exposure correlated with higher DNA methylation in the blood samples of patients with IPF and indirectly correlated with increased risk of mortality in the same population. Hence, they concluded that higher global DNA methylation can act as a novel molecular marker for increased exposure to PM2.5 [27]. Histone deacetylases (HDACs) are the enzymes that remove acetyl groups from histones, thereby modifying the chromatin structure leading to silencing of protective genes and activation of profibrotic genes. Various HDACs have been found to be significantly upregulated in the lung tissue of IPF patients [28]. Cheng et al. emphasized the importance of Class I HDACs and HDAC6 as specific drivers of the fibrotic response which can act as both markers of the disease and active therapeutic targets in the management of pulmonary fibrosis [28]. In another study, Goobie et al. utilized satellite imaging and epigenome-wide scanning to track the 5-year environmental exposure of two patient groups from the University of Pittsburgh and the University of British Columbia [29]. Two specific genetic sites sensitive to pollution were identified: cartilage-associated protein (CRTAP) and talin-2 (TLN2). Higher pollution levels were linked to reduced methylation of the CRTAP gene and increased methylation of the TLN2 gene, with the CRTAP gene being directly associated with poorer patient survival rates [29]. Identification of these pollution-responsive protein targets opens new doors for developing targeted drug therapies to treat fibrotic lung disease. Animal studies on HDAC inhibitors like Vorinostat and DNA methyltransferase inhibitors have shown promising results [30–32]. The available literature lays a foundation for designing future studies focusing on the identification of other regulatory proteins contributing to pulmonary fibrosis which may offer different therapeutic targets. The preliminary research on the role of new drugs offers future directives to conduct randomized human trials.

sec.title: Impact of Climate Change on the Environment
sec.p: Emission of greenhouse gases into the atmosphere, burning of fossil fuels, deforestation, and agricultural use of nitrogen-rich fertilizers are some of the many activities which have significantly impacted the climate. The Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change reported that, as a consequence of unequivocal influence of human activities in the past 2000 years, there has been an increase in the global temperature by 1.1 °C compared to the pre-industrial period [33]. This rise in temperature has contributed to increased climate variability with higher frequency, intensity, and severity of extreme weather conditions like heatwaves, floods, and hurricanes. It has also impacted the quality of air (altered pollen levels, severe wildfires, increased ozone levels, and dust storms) and has altered the transmissibility of vector-borne diseases and crop production. The temperature is expected to soar at a faster and alarming speed if greenhouse emissions are not checked.

sec.title: Climate Change and Impact on Human Health
sec.p: The adversities of climate change have substantially impacted human health. A World Health Organization report on climate change and health estimates an additional 250,000 deaths every year between 2030 and 2050 as a consequence of climate change [34]. Intrinsic factors like genetics decide susceptibility and extrinsic factors, such as the ability to adapt and cope with the environmental changes, indicating vulnerability of the population to the effects of climate change [35, 36]. Extremes of age, pregnant women, and patients with chronic medical conditions are more susceptible, while socioeconomically and culturally marginalized groups are unequivocally more vulnerable to the deleterious effects of poor air quality than the general population [35–37]. The complex interplay of environmental factors, genetics, and lifestyle choices can be understood by the study of exposomes [38]. Similar to a genome, an exposome represents all the environmental factors that an individual is exposed to throughout their life. These factors include pollution, diet, stress, lifestyle choices, and socioeconomic status, including how these external exposures affect the body's internal workings [39]. An exposome-based research has highlighted that a combination of air pollution and pollen, or thunderstorms and pollen, can worsen asthma [38]. Exposome-based research in the context of ILD can unfold the cumulative biological stress endured by an individual and bridge the current gap in our understanding of how various environmental factors affect the climate, individually and in synergism.

sec.title: Impact of Climate Change on Lungs
sec.p: The respiratory system bears maximum insult due to its direct contact with the environment. Factors like extreme heatwaves, dust storms, humidity, and floods are known to directly contribute to unfavorable health outcomes in terms of increased emergency visits, hospitalization, and cardiopulmonary diseases, including exacerbation of airway disorders and lower respiratory tract infections. Climate change-induced altered allergen exposure, ambient air pollution, and respiratory infections impact the airways and lungs indirectly, resulting in increased pulmonary morbidity and mortality [40, 41].

sec.title: Climate Change-Induced Alterations in Environmental Exposures Contributing to ILD
1. Air Pollution
sec.p: Combustion of biomass (wood, cow dung or burning crops), fossil fuels in engines (petrol, diesel, etc.) and coal in power stations, along with vehicular exhaust, release noxious gases into the atmosphere. These gases, when mixed with particles generated from construction, molds, pollen, etc., form PM [42]. PM is classified based on their aerodynamic diameters into three categories (Fig. 2): PM10 can settle in the bronchi; PM2.5 are found to reach the alveoli; and ultrafine PM (< 0.1 μm) are also known to reach alveoli and can cross the bloodstream [43, 44]. Air pollutants associated with significant health adverse effects include PM, nitrogen dioxide (NO2), ozone (O3), sulfur dioxide (SO2), and carbon monoxide (CO) [42].

caption.p: Classification of particulate matter based on their aerodynamic diameters and their relationship with the respiratory system

sec.p: Air pollution is one of the most significant risk factors identified in the past decade which contributes to both the development and progression of ILD. One of the earliest studies establishing a connection between ambient air pollution and ILD reported an association between the increase in the concentration of NO2 by 10 µg/m3 with an increased rate of incidence of IPF by 7.93% to 8.41%. This effect was more marked during cold weather conditions, highlighting the interplay of temperature variation and NO2 acting in synergy to accelerate lung injury [45].

IPF is known to occur in genetically predisposed individuals. Subsequent studies have provided compelling evidence on the interplay of genes and role of air pollutants in the incidence, accelerated decline in lung functions, and increased rate of hospitalizations in patients with IPF [44–49]. Based on a large UK Biobank cohort of over 400,000 participants who were initially free of IPF, 2562 incident cases of the disease were identified [46]. A linear association was observed between SO2 concentration and the onset of IPF (a 1-μg/m3 increase in SO2​ increased the risk of incident IPF diagnosis by 1.67 times) [46]. Individuals with a high genetic predisposition to IPF, when exposed to high levels of SO2, faced a 7.8-times higher risk of developing fibrosis [46]. Cui et al. discovered that similar interactive effects also extended to other pollutants. In the same population dataset, additive interactions were reported between genetic susceptibility and exposure to NO2 and PM2.5​ regarding the incidence of IPF [47]. In an American cohort of patients with IPF, an additional decline of 46 ml per year was observed in forced vital capacity with an increase in PM10 by 1 µg/m3 [48]. An Australian IPF cohort showed a 0.9% predicted faster annual decline in diffusing capacity of the lungs for carbon monoxide with each interquartile range of a 2.2-μg/m3 increase in PM2.5 levels [49]. A recent meta-analysis found that patients with IPF were almost twice as likely to have a sudden worsening of their condition when exposed to increased levels of PM2.5 [8]. PM2.5 is also implicated in the development of chronic respiratory failure and increased supplemental oxygen demand in IPF [48, 50].

NO2, CO, and SO2 have been implicated in increased incidence of hospitalization in patients suffering from IPF and rheumatoid arthritis-associated ILD (RA-ILD) [50–52]. High exposure to PM10​ air pollutants is associated with increased mortality in patients with RA-ILD [53]. Interestingly, data on the role of O3 in ILD are variable. Short-term increases in O3 correlated with increased hospitalization in patients with ILD; however, this effect was not seen with the average 30-day exposure [52]. An inverse relationship was observed between O3 concentration and the development of ILD in patients with systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) [54]. This is attributed to patients opting to stay indoors due to SLE-induced photosensitivity, thus also reducing their exposure to outside pollutants like O3 [55]. High levels of O3​ exposure are linked to a more severe presentation of systemic sclerosis-associated ILD at the time of diagnosis and a greater likelihood of disease progression over 2 years [56].

PM have also been implicated in the pathogenesis of HP [57]. It is postulated that pollutants incite small airway inflammation leading to retention of the inciting agent in the alveoli, thus facilitating the manifestation of the disease [58]. A 1-unit increase in PM2.5 level was associated with a 7% increased risk of developing HP compared to other ILDs [57].

Studies have also observed an interrelationship between air pollution and interstitial lung abnormality (ILA). After a decade-long follow-up of 6813 residents, the Multi-Ethnic Study of Atherosclerosis Air-Lung Study reported two key findings. A 40-parts per billion increase in NO2 exposure led to a 1.62-fold higher likelihood of developing ILA, and a 5-unit increase in PM2.5 was associated with a 0.54% per year increase in high-resolution computed tomography (HRCT) changes [59]. In a separate study of people from the Framingham Heart Study, increased elemental carbon was associated with 1.27-times higher odds of ILA and a 1.33-times greater odds of ILA progression [60]. ILA often represents a ‘preclinical’ stage of ILD. However, conditions like cardiogenic pulmonary oedema, obesity or atelectasis can also manifest with ILA-like findings on HRCT scans of the chest, thereby acting as potential confounders in the interpretation of the results [58].

Increasingly warmer and drier climatic conditions have resulted in a marked rise in the frequency and intensity of wildfire smoke events [61]. Wildfire smoke also releases a harmful mix of pollutants, such as PM, CO, NO2 , and volatile organic compounds, all of which are known to adversely affect human health [62–64]. Wildfire-emitted PM is known to have toxic properties different from non-wildfire-emitted PM. Some animal studies suggest that the former has a stronger respiratory toxicity than the latter at equal mass concentrations [63, 64]. This is attributed to a higher carbonaceous content, higher oxidative potential, and particle size distribution [65–68].

The pathophysiological mechanisms common to ambient air pollution irrespective of its origin include pollution-induced oxidative stress and inflammatory responses damaging the alveolar epithelial cells and disrupting normal repair processes. Additionally, they can overwhelm the body's natural ciliary and macrophage clearance mechanisms, allowing toxic substances to remain in the lungs and cause damage [69–72].

sec.title: 2. Heat and Humidity
sec.p: High ambient temperatures have been linked with increased hospitalizations for chronic respiratory diseases, largely driven by exacerbations of the underlying lung condition [73]. Higher temperatures have been found to invoke a pro-inflammatory response and to affect the permeability of the respiratory epithelial barrier [74, 75]. Animal experiments suggest that heat exposure leads to DNA damage which activates a signaling cascade contributing to the development of early pulmonary fibrosis-like changes [76]. The adverse effects of heat are compounded by the effects of humidity which promotes growth of bacteria and/or fungus along with transmission of viruses, further weakening the respiratory epithelial barriers [77]. A rise in periods of drought and hot summers as a consequence of climate change was found to increase the incidence of fungal infestation of the maple trees of Europe. The spores of this fungus (Cryptostroma corticale) released during wood processing are highly allergenic and are known to cause HP [78].

Furthermore, hot and humid air is heavier and more difficult to breathe, which increases the respiratory rate and affects ventilation in healthy subjects [79]. These mechanisms may worsen already compromised lung functions in patients with ILD.

Global warming has also resulted in floods and storms which increase indoor dampness and the growth of molds, thereby increasing the exposure to fungal antigens which are known triggers of HP. A recent study from northern Texas identified household mold as a common culprit in HP. The authors attributed this to recurring water intrusion in bathrooms, bedrooms, and air-conditioning units, emphasizing that climate-driven extreme precipitation will likely increase this risk [80].

sec.title: 3. Dust Storms
sec.p: Desertification and alteration in land use patterns have contributed to the increased frequency of dust storms, which can travel vast distances. Inhalation of these dust particles can injure respiratory epithelial cells and trigger airway inflammation and parenchymal damage. While the impact of such episodic dust exposures in precipitating symptoms and exacerbations is well established for airway diseases, evidence specific to ILD remains limited [81].

sec.title: 4. Redistribution of Aeroallergens
sec.p: Global warming has resulted in a temporal and spatial shift in the level and composition of aeroallergens. Studies suggest that new aeroallergens with unknown antigenic properties are being introduced into new areas. Although most existing evidence centers on allergic airways diseases, prolonged and high-intensity allergic seasons may also influence phenotypes of HP, in which plant-derived and microbial antigens are recognized as key causative agents [82].

sec.title: 5. Climate Sensitive Infections
sec.p: Changing weather patterns may influence the survival, reproduction, and distribution of several respiratory pathogens [83]. An association between rising temperatures and an increased risk of pulmonary tuberculosis has been suggested [84, 85]. However, evidence remains inconclusive as to whether global warming reduces the incidence of winter respiratory infections. Interestingly, severe influenza epidemics have been seen following unusually warm winters [86]. As climate change continues to alter the frequency and pattern of respiratory infections, patients with ILD may be particularly vulnerable due to their compromised pulmonary reserve and reduced immune resilience.

Incidence and progression of ILD attributed to climate change is not the result of isolated environmental factors. Directly establishing the causality of ILD to environmental exposures requires long-term studies spread over large geographical regions. The distinctive role of each of these causative agents requires assessment, while adjusting for age, genetics, geography, smoking status, treatment strategies, etc., which can act as confounding factors in the prevalence of the disease. Thus, establishing a clear cause–effect relationship is challenging due to the presence of multiple environmental confounders, including exposures that may otherwise be considered innocuous.

sec.title: Combined Environmental Exposures and Interaction Effects
sec.p: The assessment of environmental risk in ILD requires analytical frameworks that move beyond single-pollutant models to account for the complex reality of combined and concurrent exposures. Individuals are typically exposed to mixtures of ambient pollutants (PM₂.₅, PM₁₀, NO₂, O₃), indoor exposures (biomass fuel combustion, dampness, mold, bioaerosols), and climate-related stressors, such as heat extremes and flooding, which may interact through additive or synergistic mechanisms [87, 88]. These co-exposures can modify key biological pathways implicated in ILD pathogenesis, including oxidative stress, epithelial injury, immune dysregulation, and profibrotic signaling, leading to effects that exceed those predicted by individual exposures alone (Fig. 3) [89, 90]. Advanced analytical approaches—such as multi-pollutant regression models, inclusion of interaction terms, exposome-based frameworks, and effect-modification analyses—are therefore essential to disentangle these relationships and to accurately estimate exposure–disease associations [87, 88]. Incorporating interaction effects and cumulative exposure metrics may improve risk stratification, enhance causal inference, and better inform targeted prevention and mitigation strategies in ILD [90].

caption.p: The various confounding factors and their potential synergistic interactions are attributed to both the onset and progression of ILD in susceptible and vulnerable individuals exposed to various ambient air pollutants via complex pathophysiologic mechanisms. HRCT high resolution computed tomography, ILD interstitial lung diseases

sec.title: Mitigation Strategies
sec.p: Strategies aimed at reducing greenhouse gas emission, enforcing stricter ambient air quality standards, and construction practices to minimize indoor dampness warrant urgent global attention and implementation [91, 92]. Equally important are healthy lifestyle measures, such as adopting sustainable food choices, promoting physical activity over car use, spending more time in natural environments, all of which contribute to individual and planetary well-being [93].

Patient-centered action plans must be formulated focusing on reduction of exposure to periods of adverse climate occurrences. These include real-time tracking of air quality index (using mobile applications, online portals, social media), maintaining surplus stock of essential medications to avoid unnecessary outdoor trips, wearing N95 masks when travel is unavoidable, installation of high-efficiency particulate air purifiers in rooms during periods of poor air quality, and dehumidifiers to control indoor moisture in flood-prone areas.

Preparedness frameworks should be established at community levels to address current and emerging health challenges, with particular social and financial support directed toward marginalized and vulnerable populations.

On the international stage, increasing recognition of the health implications of climate change has led to several high-level summits. The 2021 United Nations Climate Change Conference (COP26) held in Glasgow was a landmark event that explicitly acknowledged the intersection between climate change and human health. A key outcome, the Glasgow Climate Pact, urged nations to “phase down” unabated coal power and to strengthen their national emissions reduction commitments, marking a crucial step toward coordinated global climate action[94].

sec.title: Limitations
sec.p: This review has several important limitations. First, the ability to infer causality remains limited. The available evidence is predominantly derived from observational studies, which restricts definitive attribution along the causal pathway linking climate change to environmental exposures and subsequent ILD onset or progression. Residual confounding, exposure misclassification, and alternative explanations cannot be fully excluded. Second, geographic and economic representation is uneven. Data from low- and middle-income countries—regions that are disproportionately vulnerable to climate change-related exposures—are underrepresented, potentially limiting the global generalizability of the findings. Moreover, this is the population most vulnerable and predisposed to climate change, including poor air quality and limited medical resources. Third, much of the existing literature, and consequently this review, focuses on individual environmental factors in isolation. This approach inadequately captures the real-world complexity in which multiple exposures, such as heatwaves occurring alongside elevated air pollution or wildfires coinciding with dust storms, act concurrently and may interact synergistically to influence ILD risk and outcomes.

sec.title: Future Directions
sec.p: Despite growing evidence linking climate factors, such as ambient air pollution, wildfire smoke, and altered allergen seasons to the onset and progression of ILDs, substantial research gaps persist. Future studies are needed to elucidate the complex interactions between genetic susceptibility and environmental exposures that drive inflammation and fibrosis in the lungs. The roles of emerging allergens and novel pollutants, as well as their interplay with diverse environmental stressors, warrant further exploration. Moreover, research must also prioritize strategies to mitigate the ongoing climate crisis, emphasizing collaboration among global leaders, health professionals, bureaucrats, and policymakers. Such unified efforts will be pivotal in shaping targeted public health interventions and optimizing clinical management for individuals at risk in an increasingly unstable climate landscape.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Climate change poses a growing threat to individuals with ILD through both direct pathways, such as heatwaves, dust storms, and floods, and indirect mechanisms, including air pollution, intense allergen seasons, and respiratory infections. The impact is disproportionately borne by vulnerable populations, such as the elderly, children, individuals of low socioeconomic strata, and those with chronic respiratory conditions, leading to worsening symptoms and accelerated disease progression.

Emerging evidence suggests that epigenetic mechanisms may serve as a crucial link between environmental exposure and the development or progression of ILDs. Hence, a dual-pronged approach is urgently required, one that enforces robust policies to curb the pace of climate change, and a second that strengthens adaptive measures to help susceptible populations better manage disease exacerbations.
As humanity confronts the consequences of decades of unsustainable choices, the actions we take today will shape the respiratory health and environmental legacy of generations to come.

sec.title: Data Availability Statement
